LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS GRANTED. THE CASE REGARDS CHAOTIC BAR FIGHTS IN MAY 2016. PLAINTIFF SAYS HE RECENTLY LEARNED NEW FACTS IN DISCOVERY AND WANTS TO PLEAD THEM. HE DOES NOT SEEK TO CHANGE THE CAUSES OF ACTION ALREADY PLED. CALIFORNIA HAS "A STRONG POLICY IN FAVOR OF LIBERAL ALLOWANCE OF AMENDMENTS." (MESLER V. BRAGG MGMT. CO. (1985) 39 CAL.3D 290, 296.) THE TRIAL DATE IS CONTINUED TO MARCH 4, 2019; ALL STATUTORY DEADLINES WILL RUN FROM THAT DATE. IN ADDITION, DEFENDANT'S REQUEST TO VACATE THE TWO MOTIONS ON SUMMARY JUDGMENT SCHEDULED FOR 10/4/18 IS GRANTED. ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS) JUDGE: RICHARD B. DEFENDANT POUR GUYS SOMA LLC'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT IS DENIED. ALTHOUGH CALIFORNIA'S POLICY OF LIBERALITY IN AMENDMENT OF PLEADINGS GENERALLY WARRANTS GRANTING A REQUEST FOR LEAVE TO FILE AN AMENDED PLEADING BEFORE TRIAL, UNDER THE UNUSUAL CIRCUMSTANCES OF THIS CASE THE COURT BELIEVES THAT THE POTENTIAL PREJUDICE TO PLAINTIFF JASON ROUSSOS OUTWEIGHS THAT POLICY, PARTICULARLY GIVEN DEFENDANT'S LENGTHY DELAY IN SEEKING TO AMEND, THE POTENTIAL PREJUDICE TO PLAINTIFF, AND DEFENDANT'S ABILITY TO PROTECT ITS INTERESTS BY PRESENTING EVIDENCE AT TRIAL OR SEEKING INDEMNITY OR CONTRIBUTION FROM THIRD PARTIES AFTER TRIAL. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Action With Prejudice
Off Calendar May-26-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Pour Guys SOMA LLC DBA Tempest Bar & Grill's motion to dismiss action with prejudice is denied.

The parties signed a CCP 998 Statutory Offer to Compromise, pursuant to which they agreed that judgment would be entered against Defendant. Defendant now seeks dismissal with prejudice. But the parties' compromise does not include an express provision requiring the action be dismissed with prejudice. (See Arriagarazo v. BMW of North America, LLC (2021) 64 Cal.App.5th 742, 749 ["under section 998, entry of judgment is the expected and standard procedural result unless specific terms and conditions stated in the offer provide otherwise." (internal quotations omitted)].) And "[i]f [Plaintiff] had wished to deviate from the standard procedural result of section 998, [he] could have proposed a general release coupled with a dismissal or otherwise stated clearly in the offer that dismissal was required." (Id.)

The clerk has set a hearing date for an order to show cause re: dismissal for June 28, 2022. As the Order states, "Pursuant to California Rules of Court, Rule 3.1385 the court will dismiss this case unless good cause is shown as to why it should not be dismissed." Defendant's motion is therefore denied.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/RBU)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication By Defendant Pour Guys Soma, Llc Dba Tempest Bar & Grill Resturant
Off Calendar Jan-17-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Joshua Moonesinghe, Esq. and Adam Braun, Esq. 310-277-4777of Braun & Braun for Plaintiff.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT POUR GUYS SOMA LLC DBA TEMPEST BAR & GRILL's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication By Defendant Pour Guys Soma, Llc Dba Tempest Bar & Grill Restaurant.

Defendant Pour Guys SOMA LLC's motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication of six specified issues is denied in its entirety. The doctrine of primary assumption of the risk does not apply to the facts of this case as a matter of law. Nalwa v. Cedar Fair, L.P. (2012) 55 Cal.4th 1148 explained that the doctrine applies to sporting and "recreational activities." The purpose of the doctrine is "to avoid chilling vigorous participation in or sponsorship of recreational activities." (Nalwa, 55 Cal.4th at 1156.) Mr. Rousssos was not engaging in a sporting or recreational activity and there is no public policy to encourage zealous participation in street fights. Morris v. De La Torre (2005) 36 Cal.4th 260 held that there is a special relationship between a bar and its patrons and a bar owes a duty to take such appropriate action as reasonable to protect a patron from ongoing criminal conduct that occurs in the presence of bar employees. There is a triable dispute whether the stabbing occurred "off premises." There is evidence that Pour Guys had control over the area where the altercation occurred. Pour Guys runs the "Box Kitchen." The fight occurred on the sidewalk by the kitchen. Patrons go to the sidewalk to order their food from the kitchen's window. (Bright Depo., 33:1-3). Pour Guys' lease provides that Pour Guys agreed to clean the sidewalk. (Braun Dec., Ex. 8, par. 27). Part of Mr. Lopez's security duties was to shoo away persons that slept on the sidewalk and/or hassled kitchen patrons. (Lopez Depo., 48:21-49:2; 51:3-8; 53:11-21.) Pour Guys even had a bench on the sidewalk. (Lopez Depo., 190:16-191:5.) There is a triable dispute whether the bar was "closed" when the incident occurred. Mr. Roussos left the bar at 1:50 an. There is evidence that the fight occurred shortly thereafter when the bar was in the process of closing down. (Bright Depo., 33:16-34:13; Roussos Dec., pars. 8-11.) There is a triable dispute whether the harm to Mr. Roussos was foreseeable. There is evidence that patrons were milling about at closing time when the first fight ensued. Mr. Bright went outside to break up the first fight. It was foreseeable that more altercations such as the one that injured Mr. Roussos could arise. It was a fluid situation. However, Pour Guy's security guard remained in the bar (Braun Dec., Ex. 5, Form Interrogatory response 12.1.) There is also a triable dispute whether Pour Guys'/Mr. Lopez's inaction was a substantial factor in causing Mr. Roussos' injuries. Pour Guys fails to maintain its summary adjudication burden on issues 3-6. Mr. Roussos alleges that Pour Guys is liable for Mr. Lopez's nonfeasance or misfeasance. Pour Boys does not present evidence addressing those theories of liability.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Joshua Moonesinghe, Esq. 310-277-4777 of Braun & Braun for Plaintiff through Court Call.
Elizabeth Beckman, Esq. 818-657-0255 of Kramer DeBoer & Keane for Defendant CMS Security through Court Call.
K. Anderson Franco, Esq. 415-268-8000 x8143 of De La Pena & Holiday for Defendant Pour Guys Soma through Court Call

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares his views re: sidewalk. Defendant CMS security argues.
The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant CMS Security, Incorporated's demurrer to the first, fourth, sixth, eighth, and tenth causes of action for negligence, respondeat superior, and negligent hiring, supervision, and training in plaintiff Jason Roussos' second amended complaint is overruled in its entirety. Per Southland Corp. v. Superior Court (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 656, 662, in the absence of ownership or possession, the existence of a duty to protect from criminal acts of third parties "will depend upon the proprietor's actual or apparent control of the adjacent property." While taken in isolation allegations about a commercial benefit from use of adjacent property, a "minimal, neighborly maintenance" of the same or a configuration of proprietor's premises requiring invitees to cross a sidewalk would each be insufficient to show the requisite control, the second amended complaint's allegations, taken together and liberally construed, sufficiently support a duty of care based on Pour Guys' control over the property where Mr. Rousse was injured. Pour Guys allegedly used the 10 feet strip of sidewalk as an outdoor patio of its business establishment. Pour Guys allegedly was aware of its customers' regular use of the sidewalk for ordering and eating food and for walking between the drinks and food area. Pour Guys allegedly encouraged such use by the way it designed its establishment and derived a substantial commercial benefit from it. Pour Guys allegedly agreed to maintain and clean the sidewalk in its lease agreement. The altercation and plaintiff's stabbing allegedly occurred on this strip of sidewalk just outside the drinks area. The second amended complaint sufficiently alleges that defendant CMS Security had an affirmative duty to protect Pour Guy's patrons based on its agency relationship with Pour Guys and sufficiently alleges that CMS Security's breach of duty proximately caused Mr. Roussos's injuries.

Order is signed.(see scanned order for details)

PLAINTIFF JASON ROUSSOS' Motion For Leave to To File Second Amended Complaint.

Plaintiff's motion for leave to file a second amended complaint is GRANTED. The case regards chaotic bar fights in May 2016. Plaintiff says he recently learned new facts in discovery and wants to plead them. He does not seek to change the causes of action already pled. California has "a strong policy in favor of liberal allowance of amendments." (Mesler v. Bragg Mgmt. Co. (1985) 39 Cal.3d 290, 296.)
